what_it_does: A running session log that records operational friction encountered while actually using the project's tools, APIs, and MCP surfaces during building and verification, along with evidence, code traces, fixes applied, and items flagged to other owners.
observations: The file opens with a methodology note from Tim framing friction-found-by-use as a gap signal that must be traced to code and driven to a fix, not parked.
It contains a numbered harvest of roughly 15 friction items from a single session, mixing backend, API-contract, MCP-tool, frontend-layout, and verification-tool issues.
Many entries are marked as fixed in the same fire with a star and a brief verification note, while others remain open and routed to fork, fabric, DNA, or host owners.
The biggest recurring drag is the need for a manual bridge bounce to make committed backend changes live, with a proposed hot-reload or fabric-triggerable restart.
Several UI fixes were verified by sight at both desktop (1440) and mobile (390) viewports, including a 0-width gallery-frame and a 0x0 channel graph.
One entry explicitly contrasts a11y-tree claims with screenshot evidence, calling out the whole-screen-verification law.
The log uses a consistent per-item shape: symptom, evidence, impact, owner, and fix direction.
standouts: Committed-but-not-live backend changes require a manual bridge bounce, serializing verification behind a single operator step.
A decision card was invisible at desktop because a flex item collapsed to 0 width; the a11y tree said it rendered, but the screenshot caught the bug.
An error banner was painting over 14 valid cards during a routine bridge bounce; the fix separated cold-load failure from refresh failure.
A channel-view graph rendered at 0x0 at desktop and with dead space at mobile due to the same two-column responsive class trap flagged for decision cards.
conventions: Markdown with a top-level heading and a numbered list; fixed items use a leading star and strikethrough for obsolete entries.
Each friction item follows a roughly uniform structure: symptom, evidence, impact, and proposed fix or owner.
Attribution is explicit: mine, fork, fabric, DNA, host, or lead-hub.
Dates and process IDs are recorded inline for traceability.
Language is direct and operational rather than abstract.
concerns: The log mixes resolved and unresolved items without a separate status column, so readers must scan for stars and strikethrough to know current state.
Some entries are long narrative paragraphs that may be hard to scan or embed later.
Heavy project-specific jargon (bridge, fabric, DNA, fork, FACE-1, MCP) is used without on-file definitions.
As a session log it may become stale quickly if fixes land and are not later archived.
notes: A session-by-session operational ledger that treats every tool friction as a code-traceable gap signal and records whether it was fixed, flagged, or remains open.
questions: What is the exact relationship between bridge, fabric, MCP, and the host runtime?
Who or what is DNA versus fork versus host in this codebase?
What is FACE-1 and how does it relate to /api/sessions, /api/channels, /api/board, /api/timeline, and /api/transcript-search?
Which of the 65 tools without form_meta are the highest-priority to document?
What is the current status of the proposed hot-reload or fabric-triggerable bridge bounce?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches — the file states it is a tool-friction log of operational gaps found by use, and the content is exactly that: by-use friction, traces, fixes, and owner flags.
apparent_themes: Drive fixes instead of parking issues
Responsive layout traps with flex and grid
Verification by real sight, not just accessibility trees
intention_signals: The file is trying to make the tool fabric self-improving by turning each build/verify session into a friction-harvesting loop: feel the drag, find the code, fix or flag it, and verify in the same fire.
novelty: It is unusual as a methodology artifact: a narrative, evidence-grounded friction ledger that records not just bugs but the operational drag of the tools themselves, including verification-tool limitations and the insight that an accessibility tree can overclaim while a screenshot reveals the truth.
contribution: A time-stamped, evidence-grounded record of tool/API friction and resolution patterns that a fused system can mine for recurring failure classes, ownership routing, and reliability hotspots without privileging any single implementation.
summary_for_embedding: A session log cataloging operational friction encountered while using the project's tools and APIs, with evidence, code traces, fixes applied, and cross-lane owner flags.
intention_for_embedding: To capture by-use tool gaps as signals, trace them to code, and drive them to resolution rather than parking them.
